description:
Welcome to Mastering Multifamily Property Cleaning!

This course is designed to equip cleaners with the essential knowledge and skills needed to deliver top-notch cleaning services for properties managed in multifamily communities. As a cleaning team lead, or owner, your role is vital in helping your teams maintain high standards of cleanliness, creating a positive living experience for residents, and to provide them with a "wow" factor when they walk in the door for the first time.

Through this training, you will gain expertise in various cleaning techniques, best practices, and customer service to excel in your role.

video.subtitle: What is a Multifamily?
video.description:
Multifamily is a fancy word for apartments. We (at PINCH) define multifamily as apartment complexes with 50 units or more. Multifamily communities have traditionally used commercial cleaning companies to clean their units after a tenant has moved out, but before the next tenant moves in.

This process has created a number of challenges in the industry, as quality has suffered for many years. Replacing one cleaner with a new cleaner typically resulted in the same quality.

PINCH saw this as an opportunity to bring high quality home cleaners into the multifamily industry to provide intimate relationships with communities and to consistently drive high quality results.

Reasons why home cleaners can seamlessly transition into this specialized field, including:

1. Attention to Detail: Home cleaners are accustomed to meticulously cleaning personal spaces, and this attention to detail is equally important in ensuring the highest standards of cleanliness in multifamily properties.

2. Adaptable Cleaning Techniques: Home cleaners are skilled at adapting their cleaning techniques to various living spaces, which is essential when dealing with different apartment layouts and configurations in multifamily buildings.

3. Time Management: Efficiency is key in both home cleaning and multifamily property cleaning. Home cleaners' ability to manage time effectively will prove valuable when dealing with quick turnover cleaning schedules on tight budgets.

4. Customer Service Skills: Home cleaners often interact directly with homeowners, honing their customer service abilities. These skills translate well to communicating with property managers and maintaining positive relationships with residents.

By understanding the importance of your role, recognizing the benefits of excellence, and acknowledging your suitability for multifamily cleaning, you will be well-prepared to embark on this rewarding journey as a cleaner in the multifamily property management industry.

video.subtitle: The Importance of Your Role in Turnover Cleaning
video.description:
As a cleaner in apartment buildings, your job is more than just cleaning. You play a vital role helping the property management, making sure rental units are clean and well-maintained for new tenants. This helps create a good first impression, keeps residents happy, and builds a sense of community.

Turnover Cleaning: Turnover cleaning happens when a tenant moves out. First, maintenance fixes any small issues. Then, a painter repaints the unit, and the carpet is cleaned or replaced. The final step is a thorough move-in/out cleaning, known as a 'turn clean' or 'turnover clean.

Property managers expect the unit to be ready for a new tenant to move in. It is very important that you complete every task in your cleaning checklist to avoid issues with the inspection.

video.subtitle: How to build your business with Multifamily
video.description:
Performing a great job when completing your Turnover Clean ensures you will continue to be asked back to do additional units. Property Managers will replace cleaners that do not meet their standards.

Grow your business through: Building a Strong Reputation: Consistently providing high-quality cleaning services will earn you a positive reputation among property managers and residents, leading to increased trust and recommendations.

Repeat Business Opportunities: Satisfied property managers are more likely to hire you again for future turnover cleanings, leading to a steady stream of job opportunities.

Many of them will request your assistance with cleaning common areas, which is highly repeatable and can boost your overall pay significantly.

These types of requests allow you to expand your business, hiring and managing more and more employees.

Referrals and Word-of-Mouth Marketing: Residents commonly ask the property manager for cleaner recommendations. Creating a relationship with the property manager and the leasing agents keep your name top of mind for recommendations, expanding your client base through word-of-mouth.

Additionally, the Property Managers share their great experiences with other sister communities and other property managers within their network.

Many times, doing great work at one community can turn into hundreds of additional jobs each year.

questions.question: What is a turnover clean?
options: A cleaning that is paid for by a tenant before they move out
A cleaning that happens the moment a tenant moves out, but before painting and carpet cleaning
A cleaning that happens after painting and carpet cleaning is complete
A spruce up or dusting cleaning after someone has already deep cleaned a unit

questions.question: Smaller cleaning crews traditionally have been left out of cleaning multifamily communities because they can't afford the insurance and don't have the back office staff to manage all the invoices
What does the word "multifamily" refer to?
options: Condos that are each independently owned and operated

questions.question: Home cleaners are accustomed to meticulously cleaning personal spaces, and this attention to detail is equally important in ensuring the highest standards of cleanliness in multifamily properties.

video.subtitle: Types of cleaning at Multifamily Communities
video.description:
In this lesson, you will learn about all the types of cleaning typically completed at a multifamily location.

Turnover Clean: A Turnover Clean is the primary service you will be performing at your apartment building. It is a comprehensive and thorough cleaning service performed after a tenant moves out and before a new tenant moves in.

The primary objective of a turnover clean is to prepare the unit for the next resident, ensuring it is in move-in ready condition. This cleaning type involves a deep cleaning of the entire unit, including cleaning all surfaces, appliances, fixtures, windows, and floors.

Additionally, any necessary repairs or maintenance issues identified during the cleaning process are addressed to ensure the unit is in optimal condition for the new resident. Typically, these cleans take place after the unit has been painted and the carpets shampooed or replaced.

Unit Touch up Clean: A Unit Touch up Clean is a quick and relatively light cleaning service performed in between regular turnover cleanings.

This type of cleaning is usually requested when a resident moves out or when a vacant unit has been sitting empty for a number of weeks or months, but the unit doesn't require a full deep cleaning.

The goal is to freshen up the unit, addressing minor cleaning needs and ensuring it is presentable for potential new residents. Unit touch up cleaning may involve tasks like dusting, spot cleaning surfaces, vacuuming or mopping, and checking for any maintenance issues that need attention.

Construction Clean: A Construction Clean is a specialized cleaning service conducted after a renovation, remodeling, or construction project has been completed in a multifamily property.

Construction sites often leave behind dust, debris, and residues that require thorough cleaning before the area is safe and habitable.

This cleaning type involves heavy-duty cleaning, removing construction dust, cleaning surfaces, sanitizing, and ensuring the removal of any leftover building materials.

The goal is to transform the post-construction space into a clean and safe environment for residents or potential residents.

Guest Suite Clean: A Guest Suite Clean is a cleaning service provided for multifamily properties that offer short-term or temporary accommodations for guests or visitors.

These guest suites function like hotel rooms within the property, and the cleaning process is similar to that of a hotel room turnover.

The guest suite clean involves cleaning the entire suite, including the bedroom, bathroom, living area, and kitchenette (if applicable), to ensure a comfortable and pleasant stay for the guests.

Custom Request: A Custom Request cleaning is a flexible service offered to meet specific or unique cleaning needs of residents or property managers.

This type of cleaning is tailored to the individual requirements of the client, accommodating specific preferences, or addressing special cleaning challenges not covered by standard cleaning services.

Custom requests may include tasks like pet hair removal, or deep cleaning specific areas like closets or storage spaces.

Common Area/Porter: Common Area Cleaning involves cleaning and maintaining shared spaces within the multifamily property, such as lobbies, hallways, fitness centers, pool areas, elevators, stairwells, grilling stations, leasing offices, and laundry rooms.

These areas experience high traffic and require regular cleaning to ensure a clean and welcoming atmosphere for all residents and visitors. Porter Services are ongoing cleaning and maintenance tasks performed by designated staff throughout the day to keep common areas clean and well-maintained.

This includes tasks like trash removal, sweeping, and tidying up shared spaces.

Model Unit Cleaning: This involves maintaining the cleanliness and attractiveness of model units used for showcasing available rental options.

These units need to be in pristine condition to make a positive impression on potential residents and help them envision themselves living in the property.

These cleanings are typically a touch up or "Sparkle Cleaning." Understanding these cleaning types is essential for property managers and cleaning professionals in the multifamily vertical to provide excellent service, maintain high standards of cleanliness, and meet the diverse cleaning needs of residents and the property as a whole.

video.subtitle: Understanding Jobs
video.description:
In this section, you will learn about the job assignment process from Property Managers. Gain insights into how Property Managers allocate cleaning tasks, the factors considered during assignment, and how you fit into the overall scheduling system.

Understanding this process will help you effectively plan your workday and ensure that you are prepared for your cleaning assignments.

What is Expected of You: Be ready for potential job assignments during your scheduled working hours. Keep your contact information up-to-date, so property managers can reach you easily.

How to Reject a Job: Sometimes, you may need to reject a cleaning job due to various reasons. Communicate promptly and respectfully if you need to reject a job. Provide a valid reason for the rejection, such as scheduling conflicts or prior commitments.

Try to work with the Property Manager on rescheduling the clean that you need to decline. If a clean is absolutely necessary on the date requested and you have no way to complete the job, Pinch may be able to assign a backup cleaner to the property.

Contact Pinch Management and we will help you arrange an alternative service provider if possible. While you have the right to reject a job, it's essential to do so responsibly and with sufficient notice to avoid inconveniencing property managers and residents.

Failing to show up for a cleaning without sufficient notice can disrupt the property management process and negatively impact satisfaction to the PINCH Brand. Honor your commitments and avoid last-minute cancellations whenever possible.

If an emergency prevents you from fulfilling an assignment, promptly inform the property manager and provide appropriate documentation if required.

Penalties for No-Show: Property managers may impose penalties or fees for no-shows within a specific time frame of the assignment. These penalties aim to ensure accountability and fair treatment for all parties involved.

Community Cancellations: When a community cancels a turnover cleaning, it means that the scheduled cleaning appointment will no longer take place as originally planned.

These situations are out of our control, and they can happen for various reasons such as unexpected changes in the schedule, tenant-related issues, or maintenance emergencies.

As a cleaning service provider working with PINCH, it's important to be aware of the company's policies regarding cancellations and rescheduling: PINCH Cancellation and Rescheduling Policy for Communities: PINCH allows for cancellations and rescheduling of turnover cleaning appointments.

However, it's essential for communities to be mindful of certain guidelines to avoid potential charges: Same-Day Cancellation: If a cancellation occurs on the same day as the scheduled cleaning, a service charge may be applied to compensate for the lost cleaning appointment.

Although same day cancellations are not a guaranteed fee to our cleaners, we do our best to collect these charges to help compensate for lost time.

Trip Charge: If you have already arrived at the community and the cleaning is canceled, a trip charge may be applied to cover the time and effort spent in reaching the location.

Use Logic and Communicate: As a cleaner, you should use your own logic and judgment in handling these situations professionally. If there is another cleaning appointment at the same community that can be swapped, you may consider proposing the swap to PINCH or the property management staff.

Swapping appointments can be a win-win solution, as it helps maintain efficient cleaning schedules while accommodating the changing needs of the community.

However, it's important to communicate any potential changes or swaps with PINCH and property management to ensure everyone is aware of the adjustments.

Always maintain open and transparent communication to avoid misunderstandings and to address any concerns related to rescheduling or swapping cleaning appointments.

Remember that while cancellations can be frustrating, they are sometimes inevitable. Being flexible and adaptable will help you navigate these situations professionally and maintain a positive working relationship with communities you serve.

questions.question: This is a comprehensive and thorough cleaning service performed after a tenant moves out and before a new tenant moves in. The primary objective of a turnover clean is to prepare the unit for the next resident, ensuring it is in move-in ready condition

This is a quick and relatively light cleaning service performed in between regular turnover cleanings. This type of cleaning is most commonly performed after a unit has been cleaned, but has been sitting vacant for a period of time.
How long should you wait to reject a job due to a scheduling conflict?
options: Tell PINCH or the Property Manager IMMEDIATELY
Let the Community know the morning of the job
You can communicate at any point
No need to communicate. Properties can read your mind.

questions.question: Cleaners have the right to reject any job.
Property Managers can impose penalties for last minute cancellations?
When there is a last minute cancellation, what should you think about or consider first?
options: You should leave the community immediately and go about your day
You should go to the property manager's office and tell them how upset you are about their cancellation.

You should look at your schedule to see if there is another cleaning at the same community. Maybe you can swap out cleanings and do the other unit first to give the property time to resolve the cancellation.
You should find maintenance staff and have them let you into the unit anyhow.

video.subtitle: Plan for the Community
video.description:
To plan for the community you are going to clean, it's essential to gather essential information that will help you perform your cleaning duties efficiently and effectively.

Here's how you can plan for the community: Understand the Business Hours: Be sure to review PINCH for proper start times.

Maintenance/Emergency Contact: This individual will be your point of contact in case you come across any maintenance issues during your cleaning tasks. This contact should be provided to you from PINCH.

PINCH Notes: PINCH may provide details about the community. These notes highlight any specific issues or concerns that need attention within the property. It may also contain specific information about a specific job or unit.

Valid ID and License: Bring a valid government-issued photo identification (ID) with you, such as a driver's license or passport. In some cases, multifamily property management staff may require you to provide identification to ensure security and safety on the premises.

Staff may also require you to leave your ID with them while you take the key to the unit. This process is designed to ensure you return the key in order to receive your ID back. It is illegal for the community to keep your ID if you return the key.

Scope of Work Provided by PINCH, LLC: The Scope of Work (SOW) provided by PINCH, LLC outlines the specific cleaning tasks and responsibilities for the turnover cleaning. Review the SOW thoroughly to understand what is expected of you during the cleaning process.

It is a "Best Practice" to leave a copy of the checklist (Scope of Work) behind in the unit. Check off all items as you complete them so the community staff is able to see that all areas have been cleaned and signed off on by your cleaning team.

Cleaners are REQUIRED for each turnover clean: Ensuring that two cleaners are at every turnover will create greater efficiency, allow you to handle heavy or bulky items, give you the ability to respond quickly to emergencies, and it will increase your personal safety.

By planning ahead and gathering relevant information, you can approach your cleaning tasks in the community with confidence and professionalism.

video.subtitle: Tools & Equipment
video.description:
Having a well-stocked cleaning kit is essential for conducting turnover cleaning efficiently in multifamily units. The recommended list of cleaning tools/supplies includes: Vacuum with Wand & multiple attachments: Wand allows you to get into hard to reach areas.

The multiple attachments help speed up the cleaning process by having the right type of tool to get the job done efficiently.

Microfiber cloths: Ideal for dusting, wiping surfaces, and cleaning glass without leaving streaks. (Consider using color coding for rags. Different colors for different areas of the unit such as kitchen/bath, common areas) All-purpose cleaner: Effective for cleaning various surfaces like counter tops, sinks, and appliances.

Disinfectant spray or wipes: For sanitizing high-touch areas like doorknobs, light switches, and handles.

Glass cleaner: Specifically for streak-free cleaning of mirrors and glass surfaces.

Scrub brushes: Useful for tackling stubborn stains and grime on surfaces, tiles, and grout.

Mop and bucket: For efficient floor cleaning, especially in common areas and bathrooms.

Broom and dustpan: To sweep and collect debris before mopping or vacuuming.

Rubber gloves: To protect hands from chemicals and potential allergens during cleaning.

Electric Drill with Bristle Attachments: This may seem funny to some people at first. However, using the drill with dish soap in areas with grout like the shower, can provide a better clean and can greatly reduce the time it takes to complete a turnover.

Sanitizing Paper: Using this super low cost item gives viewers the impression of cleanliness. Sometimes, the perception is worth more than the cleaning. Although these items are not 100% required, we find it hard to be efficient and provide a top level of service without them.

video.subtitle: Chemicals/Products
video.description:
Every cleaner has their own view on which chemicals work the best at the best price. This list provides chemicals that we have found to work the best in certain situations.

NOTE: we are not saying that there aren't other options that may work as good or better. This training is designed to help ensure your success with getting in/out quickly without having to break the bank.

Pumie Scouring Stick: The Pumie Scouring Stick is a natural pumice stone that is perfect for removing tough stains, mineral deposits, and rust from porcelain and ceramic surfaces. It is particularly useful for cleaning stove tops, toilets, sinks, and tubs, leaving them sparkling clean.

This is our #1 Recommended Product. If you aren't using Pumice Stones...you are truly missing out.

Goo Gone: Goo Gone is a powerful adhesive remover. It effectively breaks down and removes sticky residues, such as tape residue, stickers, labels, and gum, from various surfaces without causing damage. WD-40: WD-40 is a versatile lubricant and cleaning agent that can be super valuable.

Apart from its lubricating properties, WD-40 can be used to remove stubborn stains, crayon marks, and scuff marks from surfaces. It can also help to dislodge and clean off sticky residues.

Easy-Off Heavy Duty (yellow can): Ovens - This is a powerful cleaner used to tackle tough grease and grime buildup inside ovens. This product isn't cheap, so we recommend using this when the oven is in really bad shape. This product can be applied inside the oven and left for the recommended time.

Afterward, the accumulated grime can be easily wiped away with little to no effort…even in the worst conditions. Steel Wool Grade #0000 Using this product on shower glass works like a charm. Use dish detergent and the Steel Wool pad. Wipe away with a microfiber cloth.

Scrub Mommy/Scour Daddy: These are versatile cleaning products known for their dual-sided design, offering a gentle and abrasive scrubbing surface in one. They provide an effective and convenient solution for various cleaning tasks, from delicate surfaces to tough stains, making cleaning tasks more efficient and saving both time and effort.

Invisible Glass - Glass Cleaner: Invisible Glass is designed to evaporate quickly and leave no residue behind. With Invisible Glass, you can achieve a clean and clear surface without any leftover residues.

Sno Bowl: Sno Bowl is a toilet bowl cleaner that effectively cleans and disinfects toilet bowls, removing stains and mineral deposits.

Magic Eraser: This is a versatile cleaning tool that works wonders on various surfaces. It is particularly effective for removing scuff marks, crayon marks, and tough stains from walls, baseboards, and other hard surfaces.

Affresh Washing Machine Cleaner: This is designed to remove residue and odor-causing bacteria from washing machines. If a washing machine is in bad condition, this is a great product. HOWEVER, most washing machines have agitators inside them.

Our recommendation is to turn the agitator to pop it out of the washing machine. Stick the item in the sink and wash with dish soap. Great no-cost solution to cleaning a washing machine.

By utilizing these recommended cleaning tools/supplies, cleaners can efficiently tackle different cleaning challenges and ensure that multifamily units are thoroughly cleaned and ready for new residents to move in.

Properly equipped, cleaners can work effectively and deliver high-quality turnover cleaning services in a time-efficient manner.

Conversely… if you don't use some of these tools/chemicals to your advantage, you may find your cleaning teams are taking too much time to complete jobs and it is costing your business money.

questions.question: The community provides all the cleaning supplies.
Some of the items I need to bring include vacuum, glass cleaner, toilet cleaner, a de-greasing/Cleaning agent such as goo gone, WD40, and/or Magic Eraser.
What two documents are critical to remember at EVERY cleaning?
options: License/ID and a written copy of the Scope of Work (SOW)

questions.question: There MUST be a minimum of ____ cleaners at EVERY multifamily cleaning job
Why is it important to review the Scope of Work (SOW) provided by PINCH, LLC?
options: To understand specific cleaning tasks and responsibilities
To find out the community's Wi-Fi password
To locate nearby restaurants for lunch breaks

questions.question: Which cleaning product is recommended for removing tough stains, mineral deposits, and rust from porcelain and ceramic surfaces?
Which cleaning tool is known for its dual-sided design, offering both a gentle and abrasive scrubbing surface?

video.subtitle: What do I do when I Arrive?
video.description:
Be sure to follow the following steps in order to start the job fast and efficiently.

Where to Park: Upon arrival, find a suitable parking spot that does not obstruct traffic or any designated areas. If there are specific instructions provided by the property management on where to park, follow those guidelines. Try not to park in "Future Tenant" parking spots.

If you are forced to park in one of these spots, let property management know upon arrival to ensure there is not a better location for you to park.

How to Get a Key: If the property management has not already provided you with a key or access code, follow the designated procedure for obtaining the key. Typically, keys are provided by the front leasing office or the property management office. They will typically require you to leave a form of ID behind as an exchange for the key.

Who to Speak to: If there is a designated contact person provided in the app, speak to them upon arrival. This person may be the property manager or maintenance staff responsible for coordinating cleaning services.

Unless you personally sold this community, you should Introduce yourself as a PINCH cleaner. Create a relationship with property management and/or maintenance staff. This industry operates on relationships.

If the community starts to like you and your company, you will likely keep that community for many years into the future. If you simply say “hi” and go about your business…you aren't creating a relationship.

Ask questions so they know you care about your cleaning efforts and that you want to be a great partner for them.

video.subtitle: Problems with Access
video.description:
What if No One is There?

If there is no one present at the community when you arrive, follow these steps: Call Emergency Contact: If you have an emergency contact number provided by the property management (likely the property manager or maintenance staff), call them to inquire about access or any changes to the cleaning schedule.

Wait 15 Minutes and Attempt to Find Maintenance: Wait for up to 15 minutes to see if someone arrives or can grant you access. In some communities, maintenance staff may be on-site and can assist with access. Look for a maintenance golf cart or any signs of staff presence.

Use Best Judgment on Waiting Time: If no one arrives within 15 minutes, use your best judgment to decide how long to wait beyond this time. Factors like the urgency of the cleaning and your other commitments for the day may influence your decision.

Document Your Attempts: Make a note of your attempts to resolve the situation, including the time of arrival, contact attempts, and any responses received. Take screenshots of your phone providing proof of your attempts.

Contact PINCH Ops: If you are unable to access the unit or proceed with the cleaning, contact PINCH Operations to inform them about the situation. They can assist in rescheduling the cleaning appointment or providing further guidance.

By following these steps, you can navigate the arrival process at the community efficiently and professionally. Proper communication and documentation will help resolve any access issues and ensure that the turnover cleaning process runs smoothly.

What if the Unit is not Ready? At some point, you may find yourself entering a unit and Movers, Painters, Carpet/Flooring Contractors, or other maintenance staff may still be in the unit.

If the unit is not ready for the cleaning due to ongoing activities you should first assess the situation and determine if you can work around them without compromising safety or the quality of the cleaning.

Contact Property Manager/Maintenance: If you cannot work around the other trades or if they are not cooperative, contact the property manager or maintenance manager to inform them about the situation. They can coordinate with the other trades and reschedule the cleaning appointment as necessary.

Contact PINCH Ops: It is important to keep the PINCH team informed of these situations. Many of them may require PINCH to bill for additional services.

If we aren't told…we can not provide you with any additional payment/fees. What if there is No Water/Electricity/Air Conditioning? You are NOT required to clean in a situation like this.

However, you also can not simply leave the job either.

Contact Property Manager/Maintenance: Inform them about the lack of utilities in the unit. They can investigate the issue and address it promptly. Although this is an inconvenience and may suck up some additional time, this can usually be resolved by Maintenance within minutes, allowing you to proceed with the cleaning.

Contact PINCH Ops: Again…any time there are issues in a unit, you MUST inform PINCH so we can follow our billing procedures correctly.

As a cleaner, it's crucial to maintain open communication with the property management, maintenance staff, and any third-party companies involved in scheduling the cleaning.

Being proactive in addressing issues and coordinating with the relevant parties will help ensure a smooth turnover cleaning process and a positive experience for all stakeholders involved.

video.subtitle: Unit Examination
video.description:
To perform a thorough turnover cleaning, follow these steps: Open PINCH and State You Are Starting the Job: Open the PINCH app or web platform and indicate that you are starting the turnover cleaning job. This action helps keep track of your work progress and allows PINCH and the property management to monitor the status of the cleaning assignment.

Walk the Unit: Begin by walking through the entire unit to get a comprehensive overview of the cleaning tasks that need to be completed. Note any areas that require extra attention or specific cleaning needs.

Look for Excessive Dirt/Exceptions: Pay close attention to areas with excessive dirt or any exceptions that may deviate from the standard cleaning routine.

These exceptions could be stains, spills, or particularly challenging cleaning tasks that need special treatment. Open ALL Appliances (Including Washer/Dryer): Open all appliances and pull away from walls, including the washer and dryer, to check for any leftover items or debris.

Kitchen Inspection: Inspect the kitchen thoroughly, including counter tops, cabinets, and appliances.

Bathroom Inspection: Inspect the bathrooms meticulously, focusing on the toilet, sink, bathtub, and shower areas.

Blinds/Window Inspection: Inspect blinds and windows for dust, dirt, or any streaks. Look for excessively dirty areas such as blinds that may require significant scrubbing in order to clean properly.

Leftover Garbage: Are there garbage items left behind? It is expected that you will have up to one large garbage bag of trash items at each job. Anything in excess of one bag should be identified and reported.

Damaged Items: If you notice any damaged items during your inspection, such as broken fixtures, windows, or appliances, follow these two steps: Promptly contact the property manager or maintenance staff - Document the damages and report them for repair or replacement.

Contact PINCH Ops - Ensure that PINCH is aware of any damage both before and after a cleaning has been performed.

By following these steps and being diligent in your inspection and cleaning efforts, you can ensure that the multifamily unit is thoroughly cleaned and ready for the new resident to move in.

video.subtitle: Photography
video.description:
As a cleaner, taking photographs before starting any cleaning job is an essential practice that helps ensure transparency, accountability, and a smooth cleaning process.

Here's why you should take photographs and when to do so: BEFORE Pictures: (Overall): Before beginning any cleaning tasks, take overall photographs/video of the unit.

These pictures should capture the general condition of the space, showing its appearance before you start cleaning.

Document Any Issues: Take detailed photographs of any issues or areas that require special attention. These detailed images should focus on specific problem areas, such as stains, damages, or excessive dirt.

Capturing these issues in photographs helps document their existence and assists in deciding the appropriate cleaning approach and any potential fees to be incurred by the community.

Take AFTER photos: After photos/video is a great way to document how the space was cleaned and left back in the hands of maintenance or the property manager. It is important to capture these photos as commonly, there will be other trades and vendors that will enter the space after you leave.

If they create problems in the space, we want to have proof that those issues were created after leaving the unit and not something missed or caused by the cleaning team.

questions.question: When entering a unit and other trades (painters, flooring, maintenance) are in the unit working, you should...
options: Attempt to work around those trades if possible
Go to another unit or job off site and come back later

questions.question: When entering a unit, there is no water or electricity. What should you do first?
options: Leave immediately. You can't work in these conditions.
Call Maintenance or other emergency phone number.
Clean the unit to the best of your ability without water or electricity.

questions.question: Once you are in the unit it looks like you are ready to get started. The first thing you should do is open PINCH and "start" the job.
Taking Photographs is not allowed. This will become a person's private residence... so no photos can be taken.
Inspecting the unit should include both "general" pictures as well as "detailed" pictures of problem areas.

Detailed documentation and photographs protect you as a cleaner by providing evidence of the unit's condition before you begin cleaning. It can help resolve any disputes or misunderstandings that may arise during or after the cleaning job.

video.subtitle: Efficient Cleaning with Two Cleaners
video.description:
In the multifamily turnover cleaning process, speed and quality are paramount. When working as a team of two, Cleaner A and Cleaner B, efficient coordination and division of tasks are key to achieving spectacular results.

Below are the responsibilities and steps for each cleaner to ensure a fast yet high-quality turnover cleaning: Cleaner A Responsibilities: Appliances: Start by moving appliances away from the walls, allowing access to clean behind and on the walls.

Clean the exterior of appliances thoroughly including the walls around the appliances. Open the appliances and thoroughly clean the interior. Oven - Use dish soap and a pumice stone on the stovetop and interior.

If in bad condition, put Easy-Off (Yellow Can) on the interior and let it sit for approximately 1 hour.

Kitchen Surfaces: Clean all kitchen surfaces, starting from the highest points and working your way down. This includes countertops, backsplashes, and cabinet interiors and exteriors. Pay attention to any greasy or sticky spots and apply a degreaser immediately.

Lights and Air Conditioning Grills: While focusing on the kitchen, don't forget to clean all lights and air conditioning vents in the unit. These areas tend to accumulate dust and should be spotless.

Communication: Maintain open communication with Cleaner B throughout the process. Inform them when you're approaching the end of your kitchen tasks, so they can time their responsibilities accordingly.

Cleaner B Responsibilities: Bathroom Cleaning: Begin by spraying down the bathroom shower and toilet with dish soap or degreaser and Steel Wool grade #0000. This abrasive pad is effective for tackling tough stains and grime in the bathroom.

Blinds and Windows: While Cleaner A handles the kitchen, turn your attention to cleaning blinds and windows. Dust and wipe down blinds meticulously, and clean windows for a streak-free finish.

Fans and Balcony: Clean ceiling fans and balcony areas. Remove dust and dirt from fan blades and surfaces. Pay special attention to any balcony debris, ensuring it's swept or wiped clean.

Baseboards: Finish by cleaning the baseboards throughout the unit. These areas tend to collect dust and dirt, so a thorough wipe-down is essential.

Bathroom Finish: Return to the bathroom to complete the cleaning, ensuring all surfaces are spotless. Check the shower and toilet areas for any remaining Steel Wool residue, and wipe clean accordingly with a microfiber towel.

The Importance of Communication: Sync-Up: Cleaners A and B must coordinate their efforts effectively. Use verbal or non-verbal cues to signal when you're transitioning between tasks or areas.

Efficiency: Timely communication ensures that tasks align, and no area is missed or left incomplete. It also prevents unnecessary overlap or redundancy in cleaning efforts.

Quality Assurance: Cleaner B should inspect areas cleaned by Cleaner A, and vice versa, to ensure a high-quality finish and catch any overlooked details. It is important to review the Scope of Work document to make sure ALL checklist items have been completed.

Problem Solving: If any challenges arise during the cleaning process, communicate promptly to find solutions together and maintain the overall efficiency of the turnover cleaning.

Efficient turnover cleaning with a two-person team hinges on teamwork, organization, and clear communication. By following these guidelines, you can achieve a fast yet spectacular turnover cleaning, leaving the property spotless and ready for its next residents.

video.subtitle: Commonly Missed Areas
video.description:
Appliance/Area Specific Training: Tips for Cleaning Commonly Missed Areas Bathtub Tile Grout (Extra Charge): Use a grout cleaner or a mixture of baking soda and water to create a paste. Apply the paste to the grout lines using a grout brush or an old toothbrush.

Let the cleaner sit for a few minutes to break down stains. Scrub the grout lines thoroughly, and rinse with water. For stubborn stains, consider using a grout-specific cleaner or a mixture of vinegar and baking soda.

Shower Heads/Faucets: First, try dish soap and Steel Wool grade #0000. If that doesn’t work, Ffill a plastic bag with white vinegar. Tie the bag around the showerhead or faucet so that it is submerged in the vinegar.

Leave it for a few hours if possible Remove the bag, scrub the showerhead or faucet with a brush, and rinse thoroughly. Toilet Bowl (Leave Cleaning Paper): Place a cleaning tablet or cleaner inside the toilet bowl.

Use a toilet brush to scrub the bowl, paying attention to under the rim and along the waterline. Leave the cleaning tablet or cleaner in the bowl for the recommended time (follow product instructions).

Use a toilet paper or disposable cleaning wipe to wipe down the external surfaces of the toilet, including the flush handle. Put a toilet seat band on the toilet seat to show that it has been cleaned/disinfected.

Ceiling Fans: Use an extendable duster or a microfiber cloth attached to a long pole to dust the ceiling fan blades. Gently clean both sides of the blades and any dust buildup on the motor housing.

Oven Cleaning: Remove oven racks and soak them in warm, soapy water in the sink. Apply an oven cleaner (or dish soap) to the interior surfaces of the oven, avoiding the heating elements. Let the cleaner sit for the recommended time (follow product instructions).

Scrub the interior with a pumice stone, and wipe away the cleaner. Rinse the oven racks and put them back in the oven. Fridge Cleaning (Don't Forget the Top and Magnetic Strip): Remove all food items from the fridge.

Wipe down the interior shelves, drawers, and walls with a mixture of water and mild detergent. Clean the top of the fridge and the magnetic strip with a microfiber cloth and an all-purpose cleaner. Dry all surfaces thoroughly and ensure the fridge is completely empty.

Cabinets: Use an all-purpose cleaner to wipe down the exterior surfaces of cabinets. Pay special attention to handles and knobs, as they can accumulate dirt and oils. For grease buildup, use a degreaser or a mixture of vinegar and water.

Clean Kick Plates Under Cabinets: Use a microfiber cloth or a sponge with an all-purpose cleaner to wipe down the kick plates under cabinets. Pay attention to corners and edges where dirt may accumulate.

Top of Door Frames: Use an extendable duster or a microfiber cloth attached to a long pole to dust the top of door frames. Be thorough in dusting all around the door frames and molding.

Air Vents: Use a vacuum cleaner attachment or a brush to remove dust and dirt from air vents. For stubborn dirt, use a damp cloth or a duster to wipe down the vents.

Window Sill Cleaning: Wipe down window sills with a damp microfiber cloth to remove dust and dirt. Pay attention to corners and edges where dirt may accumulate.

Blinds: use a damp microfiber cloth to wipe down each slat. If needed, apply a degreaser to areas that may have accumulated stains. Room Corners (Cobwebs): Use an extendable duster or a cobweb remover tool to reach and remove cobwebs from room corners and high ceilings.

Remember to do this in any attached garages as well. Wire Shelving Try using Goo Gone and WD40 on any stubborn stains that may exist.

video.subtitle: Timing Targets
video.description:
Setting realistic timing targets for different unit sizes helps cleaners manage their time effectively and deliver quality results. Here are some suggested timing targets for turnover cleanings.

Keep in mind that the below targets are representative of a unit that does NOT require excessive cleaning: Studio / 1 BD - 1 to 1.5 hours: These units typically have fewer rooms and less square footage, making them quicker to clean. 2 BD - 2 hours: Two-bedroom apartments may have slightly more square footage and additional rooms to clean compared to smaller units. 3 BD - 2 to 2.5 hours: For three-bedroom apartments, expect a bit more time for the cleaning process due to the additional rooms and larger living spaces.

By setting and meeting these timing targets, small cleaning companies can enhance their productivity, take on more jobs, and build a strong reputation for delivering efficient and high-quality turnover cleanings.

questions.question: I am required to leave a copy of the Scope of Work (Checklist) behind after I complete a job.
Which list provides the most common areas that are missed or poorly cleaned by cleaners?
options: Bath Grout, Shower heads/faucets, Ceiling Fans, Top of door frames

questions.question: A typical 2 Bedroom apartment should take my team of 2 people this long to complete.

My team can zip through multiple multifamily cleanings in a day if we follow the following structure:

- Be Organized
- Follow the Checklist
- Work in Teams
- Use Time Saving Techniques
- Prioritize High Traffic Area
- Coordinate with Maintenance
- Maintain Consistency

video.subtitle: 30 Minute Rule
video.description:
Contacting maintenance or the property manager 30 minutes before you have completed cleaning a unit offers several benefits that can contribute to a smoother turnover process and overall customer satisfaction.

Here are the advantages of making this contact: Timely Inspection: Turnover cleaning is all about being efficient so YOU can optimize your time and pay.

Giving 30 minutes notice to management gives them the time to clear their schedule and conduct an inspection prior to you leaving... without you sitting around waiting (which costs you money!) Addressing Immediate Concerns: If there are any issues or areas that require additional attention or repair, the property manager or maintenance staff can address them immediately during the inspection.

Collaboration and Coordination: By requesting an inspection, you demonstrate a collaborative approach to the turnover process. This open communication fosters a positive working relationship with the property management, leading to smoother future collaborations.

Professionalism and Attention to Detail: Requesting an inspection shows your professionalism and commitment to delivering high-quality work. It reflects positively on your cleaning company and instills confidence in the property management about your reliability and attention to detail.

Opportunity for Feedback: An inspection also provides an opportunity for the property manager to provide feedback on your cleaning performance. Positive feedback reinforces your professionalism, while constructive feedback helps you improve your cleaning practices.

Relationship Building: The more “face time” you get with the management team, the more of a relationship is created. The stronger the relationship, the more likely the management team will come to you when there is a problem…rather than looking at other cleaning services.

We can not stress enough how important this is! This proactive approach not only benefits the property management but also enhances your company's reputation and contributes to a successful and efficient turnover process.

video.subtitle: Job Completion Photos
video.description:
Taking clean photos upon completion of your work is a crucial step for a cleaner to document the quality of the cleaning job and protect yourself against potential disputes.

Here's how to take clean photos effectively: Take Multiple Photos of High-Concern Areas: Focus on taking multiple photos of areas that are typically of high concern or have been problematic in previous jobs. These may include kitchen appliances, bathroom fixtures, blinds, fans, utility closets, and window sills. Capturing these areas from different angles helps provide a comprehensive record of your work.

Use Sufficient Lighting and Clear Angles: Ensure that the photos are well-lit and taken from clear angles. Proper lighting helps showcase the cleanliness of the space accurately. Avoid taking photos in dimly lit areas as they may not adequately represent the results of your cleaning.

Keep Photos for minimum of 2 weeks: Photos can be uploaded to PINCH. Although we encourage you to take this step, it is equally important that you keep copies of your photos for a minimum of 2 weeks beyond the cleaning date. If there were ever a dispute about the quality of your cleaning, it is important to keep back up documentation.

Someone May Make It Dirty After You Leave: Be mindful that someone may use or access the unit after you leave, which could result in some areas getting dirty again. This is why documenting your completed work with photos is essential.

It serves as proof of the unit's condition at the time you finished cleaning. Remember, clean and organized photos are valuable records that showcase your professionalism and the quality of your cleaning services.

They serve as essential documentation for both your cleaning company and the property management, contributing to a successful turnover process and maintaining a positive working relationship with your clients.

video.subtitle: Leaving the Unit
video.description:
Exiting and leaving in the right way is crucial to ensure security, professionalism, and a successful completion of the cleaning job. Here's the right way to exit a turnover clean: Lock the Doors!

Or...Be Sure It Was Left the Way You Found It: Before leaving the unit, double-check that all doors and windows are securely locked. If the property requires a specific protocol for exiting, such as using a key lockbox or leaving the unit unlocked, ensure you follow those instructions precisely.

Leaving the unit as you found it ensures the safety and security of the property. In addition to locking the doors, please make sure to turn off all the lights and straighten up the blinds.

Return the Key to the Front Office and Retrieve ID (If Provided): If you were provided with a key to access the unit, return it to the front office or designated location as instructed. If the community requires you to sign in or leave an ID at the front office for security purposes, retrieve your ID before leaving.

Close Out the Job Inside the PINCH System: Once you have completed the cleaning and are ready to leave the property, log into the PINCH system and close out the job. Update the job status to "Completed" and provide any necessary notes or comments. This step officially marks the job as finished and allows the property management or your company's operations team to review the completed work.

Additional Tips: Check for Left-Behind Items: Before exiting the unit, do a final sweep to ensure you have not left any cleaning supplies or personal belongings behind.

Tidy Up After Yourself: Leave the unit in a clean and presentable state, ensuring there are no cleaning materials visible to the resident or property management.

Be Respectful of Residents: If the unit has already been rented or is occupied, be considerate of the residents' privacy and avoid interacting with them during the cleaning process.

Communicate Any Issues: If you encounter any issues during the cleaning or notice areas that require additional attention, communicate them to the property management or your company's supervisor promptly.

By following these guidelines, you demonstrate professionalism, reliability, and attention to detail as a cleaner. Leaving the unit in a secure and clean state not only reflects positively on your cleaning company but also contributes to a positive impression of your services with the management team.

questions.question: PINCH does NOT require cleaners to contact the community staff 30 minutes before they are complete with their cleaning.

Contacting maintenance or the property manager 30 minutes before you have completed cleaning a unit offers several benefits that can contribute to a smoother turnover process and overall customer satisfaction.
If an inspection is granted, you should make sure this happens at the inspection.
options: You get a verbal sign off that everything looks great
You leave as soon as the inspector arrives at the unit
Get the person to send you an email confirming that you did the full scope of work
Do a dance or a jig as soon as the inspector arrives so they know you are happy they are there

questions.question: Taking clean photos upon completion of your work is a crucial step for a cleaner to document the quality of the cleaning job and protect yourself against potential disputes.
What 3 things do you need to remember when leaving a unit?
options: Lock the doors, call maintenance back, go home
Lock the doors, return the key, close job in PINCH
Leave doors unlocked for maintenance, return key, go home

video.subtitle: Property Manager Evaluations
video.description:
As a cleaner, your performance in a multifamily turnover cleaning is evaluated based on several factors related to quality expectations, property manager demands, and quality assurance procedures.

Here's what you need to know: Quality Expectations: Property Managers Demand Perfection: Property managers expect the highest level of cleaning quality for turnover cleanings. The unit must be left in impeccable condition to create a positive impression for the new resident moving in.

If You Miss Anything, You Will Be Called Back: Attention to detail is crucial. Any missed areas or incomplete tasks can lead to call-backs, where you may be required to return to the unit to rectify the issue at your own expense.

Consistent call-backs can negatively impact your reputation and relationship with the community. EVERYTHING CAN BE CLEANED! We commonly find that many cleaners will do their best to clean an appliance or stain around the unit.

However, their best efforts come up short. PINCH has experience and can usually help. If you find yourself in a situation where you can't get something clean, simply reach out to us. We likely have an answer or an alternative way to try to clean the item you are struggling with.

It is better to over-communicate your challenges than to say "I did my best" and then walk away. As a cleaner, your goal is to consistently deliver high-quality turnover cleanings, meeting the property manager's expectations and adhering to the provided Scope of Work.

Remember, each cleaning job is an opportunity to showcase your professionalism and dedication to providing outstanding cleaning services in the multifamily industry.

video.subtitle: Special Situations & Emergencies
video.description:
Handling special situations and emergencies during a turnover cleaning requires adaptability, quick thinking, and a focus on maintaining safety and professionalism. Here are some examples of special situations and best practices for handling them: Broken or Damaged Items: Example: While cleaning the kitchen, you accidentally knock over a glass jar, and it shatters on the floor.

Best Practice: Immediately stop cleaning in the affected area. Safely remove any broken glass and clean up the area carefully. Inform management about the incident, and take photos if necessary for documentation. Communicate the situation to PINCH Operations as well.

Offer a sincere apology and take responsibility for the accident. If needed, be prepared to cover the cost of replacing the damaged item or negotiate a resolution with the property management.

Resident Returning Unexpectedly: Example: You are cleaning a unit, and the previous resident unexpectedly returns to pick up a forgotten item.

Best Practice: Remain calm and professional. Communicate with the resident politely, explaining that you are in the process of cleaning the unit for the new resident. Offer to allow the resident to retrieve their item quickly while ensuring you maintain the safety and security of the unit.

If the resident expresses any concerns, notify the property manager or supervisor immediately.

Unforeseen Mess or Spillage: Example: You enter a unit to clean and discover that a pipe has leaked, causing water to spread across the floor.

Best Practice: Prioritize safety and notify the property manager or maintenance staff immediately. Follow any emergency procedures provided by the property management for water leaks or spills.

Avoid cleaning in the affected area until the situation is addressed by maintenance or appropriate personnel. Document the incident to PINCH Operations and any actions you take for future reference.

Discovering Pest Infestations: Example: While entering or cleaning a unit, you notice signs of pest infestation, such as rodent droppings or insects.

Best Practice: Stop cleaning immediately in the affected area. Notify the property manager or maintenance staff about the pest issue. Avoid handling or touching any pests or contaminated areas to prevent potential health risks. Follow any pest control procedures provided by the property management.

Document the issue and any actions taken to address it. Notify PINCH Operations of the incident Suspicious or Unsafe Conditions: Example: You enter a unit and the toilet has been removed. Sewer gasses are filling the air because the drain was not plugged properly.

Best Practice: Prioritize personal safety. If you suspect hazardous materials or dangerous conditions, exit the unit immediately and notify the property manager or supervisor. Do not attempt to clean in such situations. Follow any safety protocols provided by the property management. Contact PINCH Operations and document the issue.

Medical Emergencies: Example: You encounter a situation where a resident or someone in the unit experiences a medical emergency.

Best Practice: Call emergency services (911) immediately if the situation requires urgent medical attention. If you can, provide assistance within your abilities, such as administering first aid or staying with the affected person until help arrives.

Notify the property manager or supervisor about the situation. Respect the privacy and sensitivity of the situation and refrain from discussing it with others. Contact PINCH Operations and document the issue.

In any special situation or emergency, communication with the property management or your company's supervisor is crucial. Be proactive, stay calm, and prioritize safety and professionalism.

video.subtitle: On-Site Policies
video.description:
At PINCH, we have strict policies in place to prioritize your health, safety, and professionalism.

We kindly ask that you adhere to the following guidelines: No Smoking, Sleeping, or Eating at a Property: Smoking, including vaping, is strictly prohibited on any property where you are providing cleaning services.

This policy is essential to maintain a clean and healthy environment for residents and uphold a professional image. Sleeping at a job is prohibited. Falling asleep on the job is highly unprofessional and not allowed in any circumstance. Eating - Do NOT eat in the unit you are cleaning.

Do NOT use the fridge for your food or drinks. Eating should be done off site or in your car. Bring a cooler with you if you need to keep your food chilled.

Clothing: Communities expect all cleaners to wear professional clothing while on the job. Presenting a neat and tidy appearance reflects positively on you and PINCH. While there is no specific uniform requirement, we recommend wearing comfortable yet professional attire suitable for a cleaning job.

Ideal Clothing Recommendations: Comfortable closed-toe shoes with good traction to ensure safety while cleaning. Clothing that allows for ease of movement and does not pose any hazards.

Avoid wearing excessive jewelry or accessories that could get caught during cleaning tasks. 18+ to Work: To work as a cleaner with PINCH, you must be 18 years old or older.

This requirement ensures that you are legally eligible to work and have the maturity to handle the responsibilities of a cleaning job.

No Children Allowed on Site: To ensure safety and avoid potential distractions, children are not allowed on the cleaning site. Please make arrangements for childcare during your work hours.

These policies are in place to create a safe and professional work environment for both you and the residents of the properties we service. By adhering to these guidelines, you contribute to a positive and reliable image of our company and ensure the satisfaction and well-being of our clients.

Thank you for your cooperation in maintaining these standards.

video.subtitle: Go Backs / Re-clean Requests
video.description:
As a cleaner, a "go back" or "reclean" refers to a situation where you are requested to return to a unit you have previously cleaned to address specific issues or areas that were not cleaned to the client's satisfaction.

If you are asked to go back to a unit for a reclean, here are your responsibilities and how to handle the situation professionally: Remain Calm and Professional: Upon receiving the request for a reclean, remain calm and composed. Professionalism is crucial in handling such situations.

Acknowledge the Request: Confirm the request for the reclean and express your willingness to address any concerns promptly.

Inspect the Unit: Before starting the reclean, conduct a thorough inspection of the unit to identify the areas that require attention. Use the previous photos and notes from the initial cleaning to guide you.

Take New Photos: Document the areas before and after the reclean with clear photos. This documentation is essential for accountability and to demonstrate the improvements made during the reclean.

Communicate with the Client or Property Management: During the reclean, communicate openly with the client or property management. Keep them informed of your progress and any adjustments made to ensure their satisfaction.

Learn from the Experience: Use the feedback from the reclean to improve your cleaning techniques and processes. Take note of any recurring issues and make necessary adjustments to avoid similar situations in the future.

Thank the Client or Property Management Staff: After completing the reclean, express your gratitude for the opportunity to address the concerns and assure them of your commitment to delivering excellent cleaning services.

Remember, recleans are a part of the cleaning process, and handling them professionally showcases your dedication to providing top-notch service. Use this experience to learn, grow, and continuously improve your cleaning skills and customer relations.

questions.question: Most Property Managers demand perfection.  If the Scope of Work is not followed perfectly, you will be called back to the unit for a reclean.
What document should be left behind on the counter after EVERY clean?

Any trash found in the unit

options: Safely remove any broken glass and clean up the area carefully. Inform the property manager or supervisor about the incident.
Sweep it up and put it in the garbage. The community likely will not notice.
Leave everything right where it is and leave the unit. No need to clean any more.

questions.question: What are the key guidelines regarding smoking, eating, and sleeping when providing cleaning services at a property, according to PINCH's policies?
options: Smoking and eating are allowed, but sleeping is prohibited.
Smoking is allowed, but eating and sleeping are prohibited.
Smoking, eating, and sleeping are all strictly prohibited.
Smoking and sleeping are allowed, but eating is prohibited.

questions.question: Why is it important for cleaners to maintain a neat and tidy appearance while working on cleaning jobs, even though there is no specific uniform requirement?
options: To reflect positively on both the cleaner and PINCH.
To impress property managers and secure promotions.
To match the property's specific uniform requirements.
To adhere to legal dress code regulations.

questions.question: What are the responsibilities of a cleaner when requested to perform a "go back" or "reclean" in a unit?
options: Decline the request if you believe the initial cleaning was satisfactory.
Acknowledge the request, inspect the unit, and communicate with the client or property management professionally.
Immediately start the reclean without inspecting the unit.
Charge extra fees for the reclean regardless of the situation.

video.subtitle: Understanding Pricing
video.description:
For those that watched the training video, here is the Cleaner Pricing Analysis worksheet that was promised in the video. Please use this to determine pricing for any of your communities. In this module, we'll explore how to determine pricing for your cleaning services.

There are two ways a deal gets done. Either PINCH sells a community and assigns a cleaning team, or the Cleaning Team sells the deal and uses PINCH’s platform to work with the multifamily community.

IF PINCH SELLS A COMMUNITY Standard Turnover Unit Pricing: Studio or 1 Bedroom: $105 2 Bedroom: $110 3 Bedroom: $115 Additional Fees: In some cases, there is a need to charge additional fees.

Here is a listing of additional fees and when they can be applied. **SOW = Scope Of Work IF CLEANING TEAM SELLS A COMMUNITY If your cleaning company sells a deal, you have the flexibility to set the unit's price to whatever you are able to negotiate with the community.

PINCH Service Fee: 19% of each booking The PINCH Service Fee covers the cost of insurance, back-office staff, Vendor System Integration and fees, invoicing, and collections.

How to calculate the 19% Fee: Price of Cleaning X 0.81 (19%) = Cleaner Pay Examples: Amount Charged to the Community: Studio or 1 Bedroom: $135 2 Bedroom: $140 3 Bedroom: $150 Cleaner Pay: Studio or 1 Bedroom: $135 x 0.81 = $109.35 2 Bedroom: $140 x 0.81 = $113.40 3 Bedroom: $150 x 0.81 = $121.50 COMMON AREA PRICING (17% Service Fee): To price common areas, follow these steps: Determine the monthly amount you'd like to receive for servicing the area.

Multiply that amount by 1.21 This calculation is almost equivalent to setting a price and deducting the 17%.

Visual Example: Let's say you want to price a common area at $1,000 per month.

Desired Monthly Pay: $1,000 Quote to Community - Multiply (Desired Monthly Pay) by 1.21 = $1,210 Do a Math Check to ensure you are correct (or at least close) $1210 x 0.83 = $1,004.3 NOTE: This math is not exact, but will get you in the ballpark.

By understanding and applying these pricing strategies, you can maximize your earnings while providing excellent cleaning services in multifamily communities.

description:
Congratulations on completing the initial training!

Now, it's time to take your skills to the next level with our Sales Training. Don't worry if you've never sold anything before – our program is designed to empower you to confidently communicate your business story, emphasizing your commitment to quality and reliability.

If you feel confused or need more clarity on what PINCH is doing to help

cleaning companies succeed, we encourage you to watch a webinar replay. In

this webinar, we delve into how PINCH is revolutionizing the cleaning

industry and provide a detailed overview of our operations, all in an

informative and engaging webinar format. You can access the recorded session

here:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kjKcNTt999U

Join us as we equip you with the tools and knowledge you need to succeed in expanding your business with PINCH.

message: You have successfully completed Mastering Multifamily Property Cleaning: A Comprehensive Guide for OWNERS. You are now one step closer to becoming a PINCH Pro Certified Contractor. You can stop and return or continue now with the other required training module: Sales Training for Multifamily.
